original_draft.source_quote: Board completely unresponsive after being left plugged-in while off — no LCD, no LPC wakeup indication, nothing happens when the power key is pressed. LPC system controller enters an unrecoverable state; a firmware bug prevents it from leaving its locked-up condition. Try the LPC reset button (nearest the battery connector); if that fails, the only reliable recovery is disconnecting and reconnecting the battery cables.

evidence.source_quote_substring: LPC system controller enters an unrecoverable state
evidence.reasoning: BT1 is the only IC in the graph that maps to the LPC/system-controller role; the source describes the LPC entering an unrecoverable state causing the board to be completely unresponsive.

evidence.source_quote_substring: nothing happens when the power key is pressed
evidence.reasoning: The LPC produces no output and cannot be woken — functionally dead from the system perspective.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: LPC controller enters a firmware lockup and becomes unresponsive; the board shows no LCD, no wakeup indication, power key does nothing. Mapped to BT1 (LPC controller IC in graph). LPC_VCC rail would be dysfunctional; D9 (power indicator) would remain dark.

original_draft.source_quote: After a period of perfect suspend/resume cycles the LPC will not wake up the board; the LCD shows "Waking up LPC… 0%" and goes blank after a short time (~400 ms). Cold-cycling battery power resolves it temporarily.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the LPC will not wake up the board
evidence.reasoning: The LPC controller (BT1 in the graph) fails to resume after suspend, leaving the system in a dead state.

evidence.source_quote_substring: goes blank after a short time (~400 ms)
evidence.reasoning: The LPC stops responding entirely — functionally dead — requiring a full power cycle to recover.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: After many suspend/resume cycles the LPC (BT1) fails to resume, leaving LPC_VCC non-functional. The board display goes blank and the system cannot wake. Cold power cycle temporarily resolves the issue.

detail: IC BT1 sources no rail; regulating_low is silent

original_draft.source_quote: Firmware updates greatly reduce battery drain in "off" mode by making use of deep sleep modes on both the keyboard and the LPC controller. Check installed LPC and keyboard firmware versions before probing hardware.

evidence.source_quote_substring: deep sleep modes on both the keyboard and the LPC controller
evidence.reasoning: The LPC controller (BT1) is not entering deep sleep in off state, causing elevated quiescent current and battery drain.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Firmware updates greatly reduce battery drain
evidence.reasoning: The LPC is still partially active (regulating_low) rather than fully off, causing excess drain — mapped to regulating_low to represent the reduced but non-zero power consumption.

evidence.source_quote_substring: deep sleep modes on both the keyboard and the LPC controller
evidence.reasoning: LPC running at degraded/partial operating mode, estimated at 85% of nominal to represent elevated but sub-nominal rail activity.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: LPC controller (BT1) not entering deep sleep in off mode causes high quiescent current and battery drain. No rails or components are outright dead — the failure is excessive idle consumption, modelled as regulating_low.

original_draft.source_quote: It stopped working: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark. Verify cell voltages individually; reflash LPC firmware via a second computer.

evidence.source_quote_substring: reflash LPC firmware via a second computer
evidence.reasoning: The diagnostic step is to reflash LPC firmware, indicating BT1 (LPC controller) is the primary suspect for a dead/corrupt state.

evidence.source_quote_substring: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark
evidence.reasoning: Board completely inoperable with both power supplies — LPC is functionally dead.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the D9 LED was dark
evidence.reasoning: D9 LED being dark is the primary observable symptom — D9 is listed in the graph and registry.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: LPC controller (BT1) enters a dead state after deep-discharge event; D9 power indicator LED goes dark even with both supply inputs connected. LPC_VCC and the upstream 24V_IN rail are effectively non-functional from the LPC's perspective.

original_draft.source_quote: When the charge board is dead, other community posts describe connecting pins 2 and 3 on the J1 plug to bypass it temporarily and allow firmware reflashing while a replacement board is sourced.

evidence.source_quote_substring: connecting pins 2 and 3 on the J1 plug to bypass it
evidence.reasoning: J1 is the battery/charge-board connector in the graph; an open/fault here isolates the charge board from the motherboard, killing battery power path.

evidence.source_quote_substring: When the charge board is dead
evidence.reasoning: Charge board failure causes an open power path through J1, preventing battery power from reaching the motherboard.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: Charge board failure presents as an open circuit at J1 connector; battery rails BAT, BAT1, and CHGBAT go dead. D9 LED goes dark. Bypassing J1 pins 2-3 is the workaround described in the source.

original_draft.source_quote: When the power adapter is plugged in, the two white LEDs and green LED on the compute module are present; the LPC serial is interactive; new batteries report healthy voltages; all LPC commands work — but entering "1P" turns the white LEDs off and nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1. Inspect the SODIMM slot contacts for debris or damage.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Inspect the SODIMM slot contacts for debris or damage
evidence.reasoning: J2 is the SODIMM connector in the graph. The diagnostic step directs inspection of SODIMM slot contacts, which maps to J2.

evidence.source_quote_substring: entering "1P" turns the white LEDs off and nothing appears on the main LCD or CPU Serial 1
evidence.reasoning: The SODIMM slot contacts are open/broken, preventing the CPU SOM from receiving its power enable and boot signals.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: SODIMM slot (J2) has open/dirty contacts preventing the CPU SOM from initialising after the 1P command. LPC is healthy and interactive but SOM power rails VCC and VCCIO never come up. White LEDs go off and nothing appears on LCD or serial.

original_draft.source_quote: One battery cell may be leaking slightly, leaving a tiny dark spot visible on the cover. Visual inspection of cells through the acrylic bottom is sufficient to identify the affected cell.

evidence.source_quote_substring: One battery cell may be leaking slightly
evidence.reasoning: F2 feeds the battery fuse rail BAT1FUSED; a leaking cell creates a leaky-short path degrading the battery supply.

evidence.source_quote_substring: One battery cell may be leaking slightly
evidence.reasoning: Leakage implies a resistive, partial short — modelled as leaky_short.

evidence.source_quote_substring: leaking slightly, leaving a tiny dark spot visible on the cover
evidence.reasoning: Minor leak consistent with a moderate leaky-short resistance around 200 Ω.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: A physically damaged/leaking LiFePO4 cell creates a leaky short on the battery path through F2. The device cannot sustain battery operation and shuts off when the charger is disconnected. BAT1FUSED rail is degraded.

detail: IC BT1 sources no rail; regulating_low is silent

original_draft.source_quote: One USB port was not functioning properly; subsequently the kernel log filled at high speed with `reform2_lpc spi1.0: lpc_command: c/0 checksum mismatch: af expected, 0 received`. Reflash LPC firmware and check for LPC firmware/driver version mismatch.

evidence.source_quote_substring: Reflash LPC firmware and check for LPC firmware/driver version mismatch
evidence.reasoning: The diagnostic remedy is reflashing LPC firmware — BT1 is the LPC controller in the graph.

evidence.source_quote_substring: reform2_lpc spi1.0: lpc_command: c/0 checksum mismatch: af expected, 0 received
evidence.reasoning: LPC is still running but producing corrupted SPI responses — regulating_low models the degraded but active state.

evidence.source_quote_substring: kernel log filled at high speed
evidence.reasoning: LPC is active but severely degraded in function, estimated 80% nominal to represent partial operation with corrupted output.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: LPC controller (BT1) operating with a firmware/driver mismatch causes SPI checksum errors flooding the kernel log. Battery status is unavailable. No rails are fully dead — the LPC is partially functional, modelled as regulating_low. Often correlated with a concurrent USB port malfunction.

original_draft.source_quote: After reassembly the screen did not turn on; HDMI works fine; the screen flickers into existence for a few minutes then disappears — and this happens without anything touching the connector. Wi-Fi antenna placement can cause interference into the display path, though symptoms would differ and not affect the backlight specifically.

evidence.source_quote_substring: this happens without anything touching the connector
evidence.reasoning: J12 is a connector in the graph that maps to the eDP/DSI display connector; intermittent open due to misseated flat cable is the described fault.

evidence.source_quote_substring: After reassembly the screen did not turn on
evidence.reasoning: Display signal path is open — the eDP/DSI cable is not making reliable contact, causing the display to be dead.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: Misseated or damaged eDP/DSI flat cable at J12 creates an open in the display signal path. Internal display goes dark while external HDMI continues to work. EDP_BL_VCC and EDP_LCD_VCC rails cannot reach the panel. Screen may flicker briefly then die.

original_draft.source_quote: It stopped working: it was not turning on, and when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark. Verify cell voltages individually; reflash LPC firmware via a second computer.

evidence.source_quote_substring: when the power supply was connected (both USB-C PD 20 V and the enclosed 24 V adapter) the D9 LED was dark
evidence.reasoning: U14 is the source of LPC_VCC rail in the graph. If U14 is dead, LPC_VCC is absent and the LPC controller cannot operate, causing D9 to remain dark.

evidence.source_quote_substring: it was not turning on
evidence.reasoning: U14 (LPC VCC regulator) is completely dead — no output rail present.

evidence.source_quote_substring: the D9 LED was dark
evidence.reasoning: LPC_VCC goes dead when U14 fails, preventing LPC from operating and causing D9 to be dark.

original_draft.reasoning_summary: U14 (LPC VCC regulator) fails completely, killing the LPC_VCC rail. Without supply, BT1 (LPC controller) cannot operate and D9 power indicator LED remains dark even with both AC adapters connected.
